Q1: My study routine was honestly all over the place because of my work schedule and other responsibilities. I tried to dedicate about 28 hours a week, mostly late at night after everything else was done. I'd print out readings and materials so I could study during my lunch breaks or on the bus. It wasn't elegant, but I made it work the best I could given my circumstances.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and what was happening in my life. Some weeks I could stick to a rough schedule, but other weeks everything would pile up and I'd have to cram. Being 29 and juggling multiple responsibilities means flexibility isn't always a choice—it's survival. Deadlines definitely dictated when I studied most intensely.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I needed to check assignment deadlines or submit something. Sometimes I'd log in to see if there were any announcements or if my peers had posted anything in the discussions. The peer support aspect was actually helpful—knowing others were struggling too made me feel less alone. I wasn't someone who logged in just to browse around.
Q4: Honestly, the LMS never really became a natural part of my routine the way it might for other students. I'd go through phases where I'd check it more regularly, especially when I connected with a few classmates through the discussion boards. But with my limited internet access at home sometimes, I had to be strategic about when I could actually get online. It was more of a tool I used when necessary rather than a habit.
Q5: The discussion posts and peer interactions were genuinely the most valuable for me. Connecting with other students who were also struggling or who had figured something out helped me understand the material better. Reading and writing through the course materials worked well for my learning style. The videos were useful too when I could watch them, though I often had to download them to watch offline later.
Q6: I definitely ignored some of the optional supplementary materials because I simply didn't have time. When you're working with limited resources and time, you have to prioritize what's going to directly help you pass. Some of the extra readings or suggested videos felt like luxuries I couldn't afford to engage with. I always felt guilty about it, but survival mode doesn't leave room for extras.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what I'd be tested on or what I needed for assignments. Clear instructions and obvious relevance made all the difference. I also valued activities where I could interact with peers because that social element kept me motivated when I was feeling anxious about the course. If something felt disconnected from the actual assessments, I'd skip it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford, honestly. Required ones got done no matter what—I had a 94% completion rate on assignments because I knew those mattered for my grade. Optional ones would only happen if I had extra time, which was rare. There was always this guilt about not doing them, but I had to be realistic about my capacity.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download or print materials and study them on paper because that's how I learn best as a read-write learner. I used notebooks constantly, rewriting concepts and making summaries. A lot of my 28 hours of weekly study wouldn't show up anywhere in the system because I was working with physical materials away from the computer.
Q10: There were many times I'd be deep in my notes, reviewing printed readings, or rewriting concepts by hand for hours. None of that would show up in the LMS at all. Sometimes I'd meet up with classmates from the peer support group in person to study together, and that was some of my most productive learning. The LMS only captured a fraction of what I was actually doing.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a reading or video once, download it or take detailed notes, and then never open it again in the system. My limited internet access meant I had to be efficient about what I accessed online. I'd spend hours with those downloaded materials, but the LMS would only show that one initial access. It was a survival strategy that worked for my situation.
Q12: There were definitely moments when my anxiety would spike and I'd feel paralyzed about the course. When I saw my exam scores weren't reflecting my effort, I felt surprised and discouraged. Sometimes competing responsibilities—work, family stuff—would pile up and the course would have to take a backseat for a few days. Those were the hardest moments, feeling like I was falling behind despite trying so hard.
Q13: Fatigue was a big factor for me. After working and handling other responsibilities, sometimes I'd log into the LMS but just not have the mental energy to really engage. Confusion about certain topics would make me avoid those sections until I absolutely had to face them. My low attendance rate wasn't because I didn't care—it was because life kept getting in the way and I was exhausted.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd open materials and just stare at them without anything sinking in. My anxiety would be so high that I couldn't focus even though I was technically 'studying.' I'd watch videos but realize at the end I hadn't absorbed anything. Those moments were frustrating because I was putting in the time but not getting the results.
Q15: I'd sometimes postpone the harder topics because my anxiety about them was so intense. I knew they were important, but facing them felt overwhelming, so I'd do the easier tasks first. It was a coping mechanism that probably hurt me in the end, especially for exam preparation. The irony is that avoiding them just made the anxiety worse when I finally had to face them.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd structure my LMS usage around what was due and when. Without a deadline, I probably wouldn't have engaged with certain materials at all. It wasn't ideal, but with everything else going on in my life, deadlines gave me the external pressure I needed to prioritize coursework over other demands.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback motivated me to keep going, but when I got my exam scores back and they were so much lower than my assignment scores, I was genuinely surprised and demoralized. It made me question whether my study methods were working at all. The disconnect between my assignment performance and exam performance was confusing and anxiety-inducing.
Q18: The structure of the course made some things easier and others harder. Having clear deadlines helped me stay on track with assignments. But the way exams were weighted compared to assignments really hurt me—I was doing well on the work but struggling with tests. The peer support intervention was actually one of the most helpful structural elements for me personally.
Q19: The peer interaction components made engagement easier because I didn't feel so isolated. What made it harder was when materials required constant online access, given my limited resources. Also, the disconnect between what I was learning through assignments and what was tested on exams was frustrating. More practice exams or clearer connections between coursework and tests would have helped me.
Q20: I think some students might be like me—genuinely trying to understand the material, engaging with it, participating in discussions, but then struggling when it comes to formal assessments. High activity could mean someone is really trying to learn but facing barriers to demonstrating that knowledge. It could also mean someone is confused and searching for help without finding what they need.
Q21: That actually doesn't match my experience—I had relatively low LMS activity but I was studying a lot offline, yet I still didn't perform well on exams. But I can see how someone with good test-taking skills or prior knowledge might not need to engage much with the LMS to do well. Everyone has different learning needs and starting points.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone is logging in, what they're clicking on, and whether they're submitting work on time. But they completely miss offline studying, emotional engagement, the quality of understanding, and all the invisible effort that goes into learning. They also can't capture why someone isn't logging in—whether it's disengagement or just different study habits.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material and putting in effort to learn, even when it's hard. It's about showing up mentally, not just physically or digitally. For me, engagement meant connecting with peers, completing my assignments thoroughly, and spending hours with my notes even when I was exhausted. It's not just about clicking through an LMS.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much anxiety I carried about this course, or how hard I worked offline with printed materials and handwritten notes. It wouldn't capture the peer support conversations that happened outside the platform, or the hours I spent studying on buses and during lunch breaks. It also wouldn't show how surprised and discouraged I felt when my exam scores didn't reflect my effort.
